did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) deep learning model to detect melanoma and classify nine different skin lesion classes using the ISIC dataset. The method includes data augmentation via the Augmentor library to handle class imbalance and achieves a validation accuracy of 84.26%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a custom CNN architecture, data augmentation using the Augmentor library on the ISIC dataset, and the reporting of specific performance metrics (84.26% validation accuracy). Although published in a conference proceedings (EmergIN), it contains original results, methodology, and data analysis rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning-Based Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection: A CNN Approach with Data Augmentation
journal: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Innovation for Sustainability (EmergIN)
brief_summary: This study developed a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to detect melanoma and classify nine different skin lesion classes using the ISIC dataset. To address class imbalance, the authors employed the Augmentor library for systematic data augmentation, ensuring balanced representation across all lesion types. The proposed CNN architecture included multiple convolutional layers and dropout regularization to mitigate overfitting during training. The model was evaluated on a validation set split from the augmented data, achieving a validation accuracy of 84.26%. The results demonstrated that the model could effectively distinguish melanoma from other skin lesions and generalize to unseen data. The findings support the utility of deep learning methods for automated dermatologic image analysis and early detection.

all_task_types.target: Nine different skin lesion classes (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, pigmented benign keratosis, nevus, seborrheic keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification task using a CNN with a SoftMax output layer of 9 neurons. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for skin lesions using the ISIC dataset. Performance was measured by validation accuracy (84.26%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) dataset
datasets_used.description: A standardized dermoscopic image collection containing nine types of skin lesions. The original dataset contained 2,239 training images and 118 test images. Due to significant class imbalance, the authors applied data augmentation using the Augmentor library to balance the classes, resulting in a final training dataset of 6,739 images (500 per class).

use.training: Used for training the CNN model. The original imbalanced data was augmented to create a balanced training set of 6,739 images (500 per class). This augmented set was split into 5,392 training samples and 1,347 validation samples (80%/20% split).
use.validation: Used for validation during training (1,347 samples from the augmented training split) and to report final validation accuracy (84.26%).
use.testing: The original 118 test images were part of the dataset, but the paper does not report specific performance metrics on this held-out test set. Instead, generalization was tested on an 'unseen' dataset with no overlap to the training set for qualitative verification.
use.other: Used for qualitative model validation on an unseen dataset to verify real-world performance and generalization.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using validation accuracy (84.26%) and loss metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC_dataset.metric: Validation Accuracy, Training Accuracy, Validation Loss, Training Loss
ISIC_dataset.notes: The model was trained on an augmented dataset of 6,739 images (500 per class) split into 5,392 training and 1,347 validation samples. The validation accuracy of 84.26% was achieved after 20 epochs. The model also demonstrated generalization on an unseen dataset with no overlap to the training set, correctly predicting classes such as basal cell carcinoma.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation using the Augmentor library to balance class distribution (augmenting each class to 500 samples)
Rotation augmentation (random rotation with max angle of 10 degrees, probability 0.7)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were randomly rotated with a probability of 0.7 and a maximum rotation angle of 10 degrees in either direction.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Class Balancing via Synthetic Sample Generation
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used the Augmentor library to augment underrepresented classes to exactly 500 samples each, creating a balanced dataset of 6,739 total images (500 per class) from the original imbalanced ISIC dataset.

conclusions: The proposed CNN-based method effectively detects melanoma and classifies nine different skin lesion classes, achieving a validation accuracy of 84.26%.

Systematic data augmentation using the Augmentor library successfully addressed class imbalance in the ISIC dataset by balancing all nine lesion classes to 500 samples each, preventing bias towards overrepresented classes.

The model demonstrated good generalization ability and minimal overfitting, as evidenced by the small gap between training accuracy (84.77%) and validation accuracy (84.26%) and healthy convergence of loss metrics.
The sequential CNN architecture with dropout regularization was adequate for hierarchical feature extraction of dermoscopic images.
The findings support the utility of deep learning methods for automated dermatologic image analysis and early detection of melanoma.
The study highlights the importance of addressing class imbalance in medical datasets to develop effective diagnostic systems.
important_remarks: The study relies on synthetic data augmentation (Augmentor library) to balance classes, increasing the minority class 'Seborrheic Keratosis' from 77 to 500 samples; this may introduce synthetic bias and limit generalizability to real-world clinical distributions.

Performance is reported solely as validation accuracy (84.26%) on a split of the augmented training data; no metrics are provided for the original held-out test set (118 images) or external validation cohorts.

The paper does not report sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, or F1-scores, which are critical for evaluating diagnostic utility in melanoma detection where false negatives are clinically significant.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the proposed CNN against baseline models or human dermatologists.
The model was trained on a single dataset (ISIC) from a specific source, raising concerns about dataset bias and lack of diversity in skin types or imaging devices.

The architecture is a custom, relatively small CNN (6.6M parameters) trained on a personal computer, which may lack the capacity to capture complex features compared to state-of-the-art large-scale models.
Qualitative validation on an 'unseen' dataset is described only through a single example (basal cell carcinoma), providing insufficient evidence for robust generalization claims.

The paper is a conference proceeding (EmergIN 2025) with limited peer review depth compared to high-impact journals, and the authors are from a single institution in India, potentially limiting global representativeness.
